title: What State Is Your Nervous System In Right Now?
description: Discover whether your nervous system is in a ventral vagal (safe), sympathetic (activated), or dorsal vagal (shutdown) state — and what that means for your healing.

questions.text: When you think about your day ahead, your first feeling is:

options.label: Curious and ready
Slightly anxious but manageable
Overwhelmed or dreading it
Numb or disconnected — like it doesn't matter

questions.text: Your breathing right now feels:

options.label: Easy and natural
Shallow or tight in the chest
Slow but heavy, like an effort
I'm not really aware of it

questions.text: When someone you trust reaches out to connect, you typically:

options.label: Feel warm and want to engage
Feel a flicker of warmth but also some tension
Feel irritated or want to be left alone
Feel like you can't really respond — too flat

questions.text: Your body's energy level right now is:

options.label: Steady and available
Buzzy, restless, or hard to settle
Heavy, sluggish, or exhausted
Unpredictable — spikes and crashes

questions.text: When you make a mistake, your immediate inner response is:

options.label: Acknowledge it, learn, move on
Harsh self-criticism or shame spiral
Panic or catastrophizing
Nothing — I feel strangely blank about it

questions.text: Your jaw and shoulders right now feel:

options.label: Relaxed and soft
Tight or braced
Heavy and dropped, like they're weighted
I can't really feel them

questions.text: When you're in a social situation, you generally feel:

options.label: Present and able to connect
On guard or scanning for problems
Like you're watching from behind glass
Performing — going through the motions

questions.text: Your sleep has been:

options.label: Generally restful and restorative
Disrupted — waking, racing thoughts
Excessive — sleeping too much but never rested
Variable and unpredictable

questions.text: When you encounter a new challenge, you tend to:

options.label: Feel interested and engaged
Feel your heart rate rise and start problem-solving frantically
Feel overwhelmed and want to avoid it
Feel nothing — like it doesn't register

questions.text: Your digestion and appetite have been:

options.label: Regular and comfortable
Tight, crampy, or appetite is off
Sluggish, bloated, or low appetite
Inconsistent — can't predict it

questions.text: When you look in the mirror, you feel:

options.label: Neutral or friendly toward yourself
Critical or uncomfortable
Like you're looking at a stranger
Anxious or want to look away quickly

questions.text: Your sense of hope about the future is:

options.label: Present and real
Fragile — depends on the day
Mostly absent — hard to imagine things improving
Anxious about it — afraid to hope

ventral.label: Ventral Vagal: Safe and Social
ventral.headline: Your nervous system is in a regulated, connected state.
ventral.body: Right now, your nervous system is operating from what Stephen Porges calls the ventral vagal state. You have access to social engagement, curiosity, and genuine connection. This doesn't mean life is perfect — it means your body feels safe enough to be present. This is the state where healing actually happens, where learning sticks, and where relationships feel real. The work here is to notice this state, name it, and learn what conditions support it so you can return to it more easily.
practices: Savoring moments of connection
Gentle movement or walking in nature
Journaling about what feels good right now

sympathetic.label: Sympathetic: Activated and On Guard
sympathetic.headline: Your nervous system is mobilized — running on high alert.
sympathetic.body: Your results suggest your nervous system is currently in a sympathetic activation state. This is the fight-or-flight branch — your body is mobilized, scanning for threat, and burning energy to keep you safe. This isn't a character flaw or a sign something is wrong with you. It's your biology doing exactly what it was designed to do. The problem is when this state becomes chronic. Sustained sympathetic activation depletes your system, disrupts sleep, digestion, and connection, and keeps you locked in a cycle of reactivity. The path forward starts with helping your body learn that the threat has passed.
practices: Extended exhale breathing (4 in, 8 out)
Cold water on the face or wrists
Vigorous movement to discharge activation
TRE (Tension and Trauma Releasing Exercises)

dorsal.label: Dorsal Vagal: Shutdown and Withdrawn
dorsal.headline: Your nervous system has moved into a protective shutdown state.
dorsal.body: Your results suggest your nervous system may be in a dorsal vagal state — what Peter Levine calls the freeze or collapse response. This is the oldest branch of the autonomic nervous system, and it activates when the threat feels too big to fight or flee. The result is a kind of protective numbness: low energy, disconnection, difficulty feeling emotions or motivation. This is not weakness. It's your body's last-resort protection. The path back is slow and gentle — not forcing activation, but carefully building safety signals that let your system know it's okay to come back online.
practices: Gentle rhythmic movement (rocking, swaying)
Warm baths or heat on the body
Orienting exercises — slowly looking around the room
Small, achievable tasks to rebuild a sense of agency

title: How Is Trauma Showing Up in Your Body?
description: Identify the physical patterns that may be trauma responses — chronic tension, pain, numbness, digestive issues, and more — and understand what your body is trying to tell you.

questions.text: Do you experience chronic tension or pain in your neck, shoulders, or back without a clear physical cause?

options.label: Rarely or never
Sometimes
Often
Almost always

questions.text: Do you have digestive issues (IBS, nausea, stomach tightness) that seem connected to stress or emotions?

options.label: No
Occasionally
Frequently
It's a constant issue

questions.text: Do you find it difficult to feel sensations in parts of your body — like areas feel numb or disconnected?

options.label: No, I feel my body fairly well
In certain situations
Often — especially in my chest or pelvis
I feel generally disconnected from my body

questions.text: Do you startle easily — jumping at sounds, sudden movements, or unexpected touch?

options.label: Not really
Sometimes
Often
My startle response feels out of control

questions.text: Do you hold your breath or breathe shallowly without realizing it?

options.label: Rarely
Sometimes when stressed
Often — I catch myself holding my breath
Almost constantly

questions.text: Do you experience unexplained fatigue that doesn't improve with rest?

options.label: No
Occasionally
Frequently
It's a defining feature of my daily life

questions.text: Do you feel physically unsafe in your own body — like your body is the threat rather than the refuge?

options.label: No, my body feels like home
Sometimes I feel uncomfortable in my body
Often — I'd rather not be in my body
My body feels like enemy territory

questions.text: Do you have difficulty tolerating physical touch — even gentle, safe touch?

options.label: No, touch feels generally okay
Depends on who it is
Often uncomfortable, even with people I trust
Touch feels threatening or overwhelming

questions.text: Do you experience physical symptoms (headaches, skin issues, immune problems) that doctors can't fully explain?

options.label: No
Occasionally
Yes, a few recurring issues
Multiple unexplained symptoms are a constant

questions.text: When you're emotionally triggered, does your body respond before your mind catches up?

options.label: Rarely — I usually notice thoughts first
Sometimes
Often — my body reacts and I figure out why later
Always — my body is way ahead of my awareness

low.label: Minimal Somatic Impact
low.headline: Your body is carrying relatively few trauma signals right now.
low.body: Your responses suggest your body is not showing strong signs of stored trauma at this time. That's genuinely good news. It could mean you've done significant healing work, that your nervous system has good regulation capacity, or that you haven't experienced significant trauma. Whatever the reason, this is a strong foundation. The work now is maintenance and deepening — continuing to build body awareness and nervous system literacy so you can recognize early signals before they become chronic patterns.
practices: Regular body scan practice
Preventive somatic check-ins
Building body literacy through reading and practice

moderate.label: Moderate Somatic Impact
moderate.headline: Your body is holding some trauma patterns — and they're worth paying attention to.
moderate.body: Your responses suggest a moderate level of somatic trauma impact. You're experiencing some of the classic body-based signs: tension patterns, digestive sensitivity, startle responses, or disconnection from sensation. These aren't random. They're your body's intelligent adaptations to experiences that felt threatening. The good news is that at this level, somatic work tends to be very effective. Your nervous system still has significant capacity for regulation, and body-based practices can create real change relatively quickly.
practices: Somatic Experiencing or body-based therapy
Daily breathwork (extended exhale)
Grounding practices — especially feet-on-floor awareness
TRE exercises

high.label: Significant Somatic Impact
high.headline: Your body is carrying significant trauma — and it deserves real, skilled attention.
high.body: Your responses suggest your body is holding a significant amount of trauma. The patterns you're experiencing — chronic pain, disconnection, digestive issues, touch sensitivity, unexplained symptoms — are not character flaws or signs of weakness. They're the body's way of encoding experiences that were too much to process at the time. This level of somatic impact typically benefits most from working with a skilled somatic therapist who can help you approach this material safely, at the right pace. You don't have to do this alone, and you shouldn't have to.
practices: Find a somatic therapist (SE, Sensorimotor, or EMDR)
Gentle, trauma-sensitive yoga
Very slow, titrated body awareness practices
Build safety before processing

title: How Wide Is Your Window of Tolerance?
description: Assess your capacity to stay present with difficult emotions and sensations without going into overwhelm (hyperarousal) or shutdown (hypoarousal).

questions.text: When you feel a strong emotion, you can usually:

options.label: Stay with it and let it move through
Stay with it briefly before needing to distract
Feel it briefly before it overwhelms you
Either go numb or completely lose it

questions.text: When something stressful happens, you return to baseline:

options.label: Within minutes to an hour
Within a few hours
It takes a day or more
I'm not sure I have a baseline to return to

questions.text: When someone raises their voice near you, you:

options.label: Stay grounded and respond thoughtfully
Feel activated but can manage it
Freeze, panic, or get very upset
Dissociate or shut down completely

questions.text: Your capacity to tolerate uncertainty is:

options.label: Generally okay — you can sit with not knowing
Manageable but uncomfortable
Very difficult — uncertainty triggers anxiety
Intolerable — you need to know or you fall apart

questions.text: When you're in a conflict with someone you care about, you can usually:

options.label: Stay present and work through it
Get activated but come back to the conversation
Escalate quickly or shut down
Completely lose yourself in the reaction

questions.text: Your ability to be present in your body during everyday activities is:

options.label: Generally good
Okay most of the time
Often difficult — you're frequently in your head
Very limited — you feel disconnected most of the time

questions.text: When you think about a past difficult experience, you:

options.label: Can reflect on it without being overwhelmed
Feel some activation but can stay present
Feel flooded or very distressed
Either go numb or feel like it's happening again

questions.text: Your capacity to experience joy, pleasure, or playfulness is:

options.label: Available and real
Present but sometimes feels forced
Limited — hard to access
Mostly absent

wide.label: Wide Window of Tolerance
wide.headline: You have a well-developed capacity to stay present with difficult experiences.
wide.body: Your window of tolerance is wide — meaning you can experience a range of emotions and sensations without being overwhelmed or shutting down. This is a significant resource. It suggests either natural resilience, effective healing work, or both. A wide window doesn't mean you don't have hard experiences. It means your nervous system has the capacity to process them. The work here is to keep expanding this capacity and to use it in service of deeper healing.
practices: Deepen your practice with more challenging material
Support others — your regulation can co-regulate those around you
Explore more advanced somatic practices

moderate.label: Moderate Window of Tolerance
moderate.headline: Your window is workable — with some edges that need attention.
moderate.body: Your window of tolerance is moderate. You have real capacity to stay present, but there are edges where you tip into overwhelm or shutdown. This is very common, and it's workable. The goal isn't to never get activated — it's to expand the range of what you can tolerate while staying present. Somatic practices, breathwork, and titrated trauma work can all help widen this window over time.
practices: Daily grounding and regulation practice
Pendulation — moving between activation and resource
Breathwork for regulation
Somatic therapy to work the edges safely

narrow.label: Narrow Window of Tolerance
narrow.headline: Your window is currently narrow — and that's important information, not a verdict.
narrow.body: Your window of tolerance appears narrow right now. This means your nervous system is frequently tipping into either overwhelm (hyperarousal) or shutdown (hypoarousal), with limited time in the regulated middle zone. This is not a permanent state. Windows of tolerance can be widened with the right support and the right pacing. The key word is pacing — trying to push through too fast will narrow the window further. The work starts with building safety and resources, not with processing trauma.
practices: Start with resourcing — not trauma processing
Find a skilled somatic therapist
Very gentle grounding practices daily
Reduce unnecessary stressors where possible

title: Is People-Pleasing a Trauma Response for You?
description: Explore whether the fawn response — chronic people-pleasing, difficulty with boundaries, and self-abandonment — is operating as a trauma survival strategy in your life.

questions.text: When someone seems upset or displeased with you, your first instinct is to:

options.label: Check in with yourself first, then respond
Want to fix it but can usually hold your ground
Immediately try to smooth things over
Panic and do whatever it takes to restore approval

questions.text: Saying 'no' to someone you care about feels:

options.label: Normal — you can do it without much distress
Uncomfortable but manageable
Very difficult — you often say yes when you mean no
Nearly impossible — the anxiety is too intense

questions.text: In conflict situations, you tend to:

options.label: Stay present and work toward resolution
Get anxious but can hold your position
Quickly concede to restore peace
Immediately take the blame to end the conflict

questions.text: How often do you find yourself agreeing with people even when you don't actually agree?

options.label: Rarely
Sometimes in high-stakes situations
Often — it feels safer
Almost always — I don't know what I actually think

questions.text: When someone is angry — even if not at you — you feel:

options.label: Alert but not destabilized
Uncomfortable and want to leave
Compelled to intervene and calm things down
Terrified and immediately try to appease

questions.text: Your sense of your own needs and preferences is:

options.label: Clear and accessible
Generally clear but sometimes hard to prioritize
Often unclear — you're not sure what you want
Very unclear — other people's needs always come first

questions.text: After spending time with others, you often feel:

options.label: Energized or neutral
Tired but okay
Drained — like you gave too much
Empty and resentful — you performed the whole time

questions.text: When you receive criticism, even constructive feedback, you:

options.label: Can hear it and consider it
Feel stung but recover
Feel deeply ashamed and want to fix it immediately
Spiral into self-blame and over-apologizing

questions.text: Do you find yourself monitoring other people's moods and adjusting your behavior accordingly?

options.label: Occasionally, as a normal social skill
Sometimes, especially with certain people
Often — you're always reading the room
Constantly — it's exhausting but automatic

questions.text: The idea of someone being disappointed in you feels:

options.label: Unfortunate but manageable
Uncomfortable but not catastrophic
Very distressing — you work hard to avoid it
Intolerable — like a threat to your safety

low.label: Minimal Fawn Patterns
low.headline: People-pleasing doesn't appear to be a dominant pattern for you.
low.body: Your responses suggest you have a relatively healthy relationship with your own needs and boundaries. You can generally say no, hold your position in conflict, and tolerate others' disappointment without it destabilizing you. This is a real resource. It doesn't mean you never accommodate others — it means you do so from choice rather than fear.
practices: Continue building self-awareness
Notice any situations where fawn patterns do emerge
Celebrate your capacity for healthy boundaries

moderate.label: Moderate Fawn Patterns
moderate.headline: People-pleasing is present — and it's worth exploring where it comes from.
moderate.body: Your responses suggest moderate fawn patterns. You have some capacity to hold your own needs, but there are situations where the pull to appease, agree, or smooth things over overrides your own truth. This is very common, especially for people who grew up in environments where expressing needs felt dangerous. The fawn response was smart once. The work is learning that you don't need it anymore — that your safety doesn't depend on other people's approval.
practices: Practice small acts of boundary-setting
Notice the physical sensation of fawn activation
Explore the early origins of this pattern
Work with a therapist on parts work or IFS

high.label: Strong Fawn Patterns
high.headline: People-pleasing appears to be a central organizing strategy for you — and it's costing you.
high.body: Your responses suggest strong fawn patterns. The fawn response is running your show — monitoring others' moods, suppressing your own needs, agreeing when you don't agree, and treating others' approval as a survival requirement. This is exhausting. And it makes sense. Somewhere along the way, your nervous system learned that keeping others happy was the safest strategy. That was probably true then. It's not true now. But your body doesn't know that yet. This is exactly the kind of pattern that somatic and parts-based work can address.
practices: Find a trauma-informed therapist (IFS, somatic, or EMDR)
Start noticing — not changing — the fawn activation first
Practice the pause: one breath before responding to requests
Read about fawn response and attachment trauma

title: Are You Ready to Start Somatic Healing Work?
description: Assess your current readiness for body-based trauma work — including your stability, resources, and what kind of support would serve you best right now.

questions.text: Your current life circumstances are:

options.label: Relatively stable — no major active crises
Manageable — some stress but functional
Quite stressful — multiple demands
In crisis or highly destabilized

questions.text: You have at least one person in your life you can genuinely rely on for support:

options.label: Yes, several people
Yes, one or two
Maybe one, but it's complicated
Not really

questions.text: Your relationship with your body right now is:

options.label: Curious and willing to explore
Cautious but open
Uncomfortable — you'd rather not focus on it
Actively hostile or terrifying

questions.text: You have some capacity to tolerate uncomfortable sensations without immediately needing to escape them:

options.label: Yes, reasonably well
Sometimes, with effort
Rarely — discomfort feels overwhelming
No — any discomfort triggers panic or shutdown

questions.text: You have access to at least one thing that reliably helps you feel calmer or safer:

options.label: Yes, several reliable resources
Yes, one or two things
Uncertain — things that used to help don't always work
No — nothing feels reliably calming

questions.text: Your motivation for doing somatic work is:

options.label: Genuine curiosity and desire to heal
Real but mixed with fear
Mostly external pressure (someone else thinks I should)
I'm not sure I want to do this

questions.text: You can generally function in daily life — work, relationships, basic self-care:

options.label: Yes, fairly well
With effort and some support
Struggling significantly in one or more areas
Basic functioning is very difficult right now

questions.text: You have some understanding of what somatic therapy involves:

options.label: Yes, I've researched it and understand the basics
Some understanding
Very little — I'm mostly going on instinct
No idea — I just know I need help

questions.text: You're willing to go slowly — to prioritize safety over speed in your healing:

options.label: Yes, I understand why that matters
Mostly, though I want results
It's hard — I want to be done with this
No — I need this to be over fast

questions.text: You have some financial or practical access to therapeutic support:

options.label: Yes, I can access therapy
Limited but possible
Very limited — I'd need low-cost options
Not currently accessible to me

ready.label: Ready to Begin
ready.headline: You have a strong foundation for somatic healing work.
ready.body: Your responses suggest you're in a good position to begin or deepen somatic healing work. You have stability, some resources, motivation, and a willingness to go slowly. These are the real prerequisites — not having everything figured out, but having enough ground under your feet to do the work safely. The next step is finding the right practitioner and approach for your specific needs.
practices: Research somatic therapists in your area
Start with a consultation to find the right fit
Begin building daily somatic practices now
Read foundational books on somatic healing

building.label: Building Readiness
building.headline: You're on your way — with some foundations still to build.
building.body: Your responses suggest you have some readiness for somatic work, but there are areas where more foundation-building would help. This isn't a reason to wait indefinitely — it's a reason to be thoughtful about where you start. Self-directed practices, psychoeducation, and building your support network are all valuable first steps. A skilled somatic therapist can also help you build the foundations you need as part of the work itself.
practices: Focus on stabilization and resourcing first
Build your support network
Start with gentle self-directed somatic practices
Consider a therapist who specializes in stabilization

stabilize.label: Stabilize First
stabilize.headline: Right now, stabilization is more important than trauma processing.
stabilize.body: Your responses suggest that before diving into trauma processing work, the priority is stabilization. This means building safety, resources, and daily functioning. This is not a failure — it's wisdom. Trying to process trauma when your foundation is unstable often makes things worse, not better. The most important thing you can do right now is get support for your current level of distress, build one or two reliable calming strategies, and reduce unnecessary stressors where possible.
practices: Seek crisis support if needed
Focus on basic safety and daily functioning
Build one reliable calming practice
Consider a therapist focused on stabilization, not processing

title: How Childhood Trauma Shows Up in Your Adult Body
description: Adverse childhood experiences leave lasting imprints on the body and nervous system. Identify the patterns your body may be carrying from early experiences.

questions.text: Do you have difficulty trusting that good things in your life will last?

options.label: Rarely — I can generally enjoy good things
Sometimes — I wait for the other shoe to drop
Often — good things feel fragile or undeserved
Always — I can't let myself believe good things are real

questions.text: How do you respond to authority figures or people with power over you?

options.label: Generally fine — I can engage with them comfortably
Some anxiety but manageable
I feel anxious and try to please them or become invisible
I feel small, young, or like I'm back in childhood

questions.text: Do you experience a harsh inner critic — a voice that attacks or shames you?

options.label: Rarely — my inner voice is generally kind
Sometimes, especially after mistakes
Often — the critic is loud and cruel
Almost constantly — it's the dominant voice in my head

questions.text: Do you feel a deep sense of shame — not just about things you've done, but about who you are?

options.label: Rarely — I generally feel okay about myself
Sometimes, especially when I make mistakes
Often — I feel fundamentally flawed or broken
It's a constant undercurrent — I feel deeply wrong at my core

questions.text: How do you experience intimacy and closeness in relationships?

options.label: Generally okay — I can experience closeness without too much fear
I want closeness but feel anxious when I get it
Closeness feels dangerous — I push people away or cling too tightly
Intimacy feels impossible or terrifying

questions.text: When you're triggered, do you ever feel like you're reliving a childhood experience?

options.label: Rarely — I generally stay in the present
Sometimes — certain situations feel disproportionately intense
Often — I can feel young, small, or like I'm back in the past
Frequently — I lose track of the present entirely

questions.text: Do you have a stable sense of who you are — your values, preferences, and identity?

options.label: Yes — I have a fairly clear sense of myself
Mostly, but it shifts in certain relationships
Not really — my sense of self shifts depending on who I'm with
I feel like I don't know who I am at all

questions.text: How do you respond to your own pain or suffering?

options.label: With some compassion — I try to take care of myself
I push through it — I tell myself to toughen up
I feel like I deserve to suffer
I feel numb to my own pain — it doesn't register

questions.text: Do you have a sense of your own worth independent of what you do or achieve?

options.label: Yes — I feel worthwhile just as I am
I know it intellectually but don't always feel it
My worth feels entirely conditional on performance or approval
I don't feel I have much worth at all

questions.text: Do you have difficulty identifying or expressing your emotions?

options.label: No — I have good emotional awareness
Sometimes — certain emotions are easier than others
Often — I don't know what I'm feeling
Almost always — emotions feel inaccessible or overwhelming

low.label: Minimal Childhood Trauma Patterns
low.headline: Your responses suggest relatively few childhood trauma patterns.
low.body: Your responses suggest that childhood trauma is not significantly shaping your current experience. This could mean you had a relatively stable early environment, that you've done significant healing work, or that your resilience factors were strong. Whatever the reason, this is a genuine resource. The work here is to maintain and deepen your wellbeing — building body awareness, healthy relationships, and continued self-understanding.
practices: Continue practices that support your wellbeing
Build deeper body awareness
Explore somatic practices to deepen your connection to yourself

moderate.label: Moderate Childhood Trauma Patterns
moderate.headline: Childhood experiences are showing up in your adult patterns.
moderate.body: Your responses suggest that childhood experiences are shaping some of your current patterns — in your relationships, your sense of self, your inner critic, or your body. This is incredibly common. Most people carry some imprint of their early experiences. The important thing is that these patterns can change. With the right support and approaches, the nervous system can learn new ways of being that weren't available in childhood.
practices: Explore therapy focused on childhood trauma and attachment
Practice self-compassion as a somatic experience
Read about complex PTSD to understand your patterns in context

high.label: Significant Childhood Trauma Patterns
high.headline: Childhood trauma appears to be significantly shaping your current experience.
high.body: Your responses suggest that childhood trauma is significantly shaping your current experience — your sense of self, your relationships, your inner critic, your body, and your capacity for joy and connection. This is not a life sentence. These patterns developed for good reasons — they helped you survive. But they no longer serve you, and they can change. Complex trauma requires skilled, specialized support. You deserve a therapist who understands the depth and complexity of what you're carrying.
practices: Seek a therapist who specializes in complex trauma and C-PTSD
Consider somatic approaches that address body-level imprints
Be patient with yourself — complex trauma healing takes time but is real

title: What's Your Polyvagal Profile?
description: Based on polyvagal theory, discover which autonomic state you spend the most time in — and how it shapes your health, relationships, and capacity for healing.

questions.text: How do you typically feel in your body during a normal day?

options.label: Generally at ease, present, and connected
Tense, alert, and ready for something to go wrong
Heavy, foggy, flat, or disconnected
It shifts dramatically throughout the day

questions.text: How do you experience your voice when talking to others?

options.label: Warm, expressive, and connected
Tense, tight, or I speak quickly when stressed
Flat, monotone, or I lose my voice under pressure
It varies a lot depending on my state

questions.text: How do you experience listening to others?

options.label: I can listen with full attention and feel genuinely engaged
I'm listening but also scanning for threats or problems
It's hard to stay present — I drift or go blank
I hear the words but have trouble connecting to the meaning

questions.text: How do you experience your digestion?

options.label: Generally fine — no major issues
Reactive — nausea, cramping, or IBS-type symptoms under stress
Sluggish, slow, or chronic digestive issues
Unpredictable — I can't tell what will set it off

questions.text: How do you experience your heart rate?

options.label: Generally steady and calm
Frequently elevated, racing, or pounding
Slow, heavy, or I sometimes feel faint
I don't notice my heart rate much

questions.text: How do you experience your breath?

options.label: Generally full and easy
Shallow, tight, or I hold my breath without realizing it
Very shallow, slow, or I feel like I'm not breathing enough
I don't pay attention to my breath

questions.text: How do you experience your sense of connection to others?

options.label: Generally connected — I feel part of something larger
Guarded — I want connection but it feels risky
Isolated — I feel fundamentally alone even with others
Disconnected from everyone, including myself

questions.text: How do you experience your immune system and overall health?

options.label: Generally healthy — I get sick occasionally but recover well
I get sick frequently or have chronic inflammatory issues
Chronic fatigue, autoimmune issues, or feel generally unwell
I'm not sure — I don't track this

questions.text: How do you experience joy or pleasure?

options.label: I can feel genuine joy and let it land in my body
Joy quickly turns to anxiety — I wait for something bad to happen
Joy feels flat or inaccessible to me right now
I experience joy but always with a layer of guilt or unworthiness

questions.text: How do you experience your sense of safety in the world?

options.label: The world feels generally safe enough
I'm constantly scanning for threats — the world feels dangerous
The world feels pointless or overwhelming — I've given up trying
Safety feels conditional — I'm okay only when things are controlled

ventral.label: Ventral Vagal Dominant
ventral.headline: Your autonomic profile is primarily ventral vagal — the state of safety and connection.
ventral.body: You spend much of your time in the ventral vagal state — the social engagement system that Stephen Porges identified as the foundation of health, healing, and genuine connection. This is the state where digestion works, immunity is strong, voice is warm, and relationships feel real. Your nervous system has enough capacity to engage with life from a place of relative safety. This is a genuine resource — not just for yourself, but for everyone around you, since regulated nervous systems help regulate others.
practices: Use your ventral vagal capacity as a resource in healing
Practice co-regulation — your regulated nervous system can help regulate others
Explore somatic practices to deepen your ventral vagal access

sympathetic.label: Sympathetic Dominant
sympathetic.headline: Your autonomic profile is primarily sympathetic — fight-or-flight is your default.
sympathetic.body: You spend much of your time in sympathetic activation — the fight-or-flight branch of the autonomic nervous system. Your body is working hard to keep you safe, scanning for threats, mobilizing energy, keeping you on guard. This is not a character flaw. It's your nervous system doing exactly what it evolved to do. The problem is when this state becomes chronic. Sustained sympathetic activation depletes your system, disrupts sleep, digestion, and immunity, and keeps you locked in a cycle of reactivity that makes genuine connection and healing very difficult.
practices: Vagal toning: humming, gargling, singing
Physiological sigh breathing (double inhale, long exhale)
Cold water on face or wrists to trigger the dive reflex
Somatic therapy to discharge stored sympathetic activation

dorsal.label: Dorsal Vagal Dominant
dorsal.headline: Your autonomic profile is primarily dorsal vagal — shutdown is your default.
dorsal.body: You spend much of your time in dorsal vagal shutdown — the freeze, collapse, or immobilization state. This is your nervous system's most ancient survival response, activating when the threat feels too big to fight or flee. The result is a kind of protective numbness: low energy, disconnection, difficulty feeling emotions or motivation, chronic fatigue. This is not laziness or depression (though it can look like both). It's your body's last-resort protection. The path back is slow and gentle — not forcing activation, but carefully building safety signals that let your system know it's okay to come back online.
practices: Gentle rhythmic movement: rocking, swaying, slow walking
Warm baths or heat on the body
Orienting exercises — slowly looking around the room
Seek co-regulation with safe, regulated people

title: Is EMDR Right for You?
description: EMDR is one of the most evidence-based trauma treatments available. Find out if you're a good candidate, what to expect, and what preparation you might need first.

questions.text: Do you have specific traumatic memories or events that continue to disturb you?

options.label: Yes — specific memories that feel unresolved and intrusive
More of a general sense of trauma without specific memories
I have difficult experiences but I'm not sure they're 'traumatic'
I have chronic stress but no specific trauma events

questions.text: When you think about a difficult memory, what typically happens?

options.label: I feel the emotions and body sensations as if it's happening now
I feel some distress but can stay present
I go numb or blank — I can't access the memory emotionally
I avoid thinking about it entirely

questions.text: How stable is your life right now?

options.label: Fairly stable — I have support and basic safety
Somewhat stable but with significant ongoing stressors
In active crisis or significant instability
Stable externally but internally very dysregulated

questions.text: Do you have effective ways to calm and ground yourself when overwhelmed?

options.label: Yes — I have reliable regulation strategies
Some things help but they're not always reliable
No — I don't know how to regulate when I'm overwhelmed
I have strategies but I forget to use them when triggered

questions.text: Have you experienced significant dissociation (losing time, feeling detached, not in your body)?

options.label: Rarely or mildly
Sometimes, especially when triggered
Frequently — dissociation is a significant part of my experience
I'm not sure what dissociation is

questions.text: Have you worked with a therapist before?

options.label: Yes, and it was helpful
Yes, but talking alone wasn't enough — I want something deeper
No, this would be my first time
I've tried but couldn't continue for various reasons

questions.text: How do you feel about the idea of bilateral stimulation (eye movements, tapping, or sounds) during therapy?

options.label: Curious and open to trying it
Skeptical but willing to try if there's evidence
Uncomfortable — it sounds strange
I've heard about it and I'm interested

questions.text: What is your primary goal for trauma therapy?

options.label: To process specific traumatic memories so they no longer disturb me
To understand why I feel the way I do
To build better coping skills and regulation
To heal my relationship with myself and others

strong.label: Strong EMDR Candidate
strong.headline: You appear to be a strong candidate for EMDR therapy.
strong.body: Your responses suggest you have the stability, specific memories, and openness to benefit significantly from EMDR. EMDR works best when you have identifiable disturbing memories, enough stability to engage with the process, and some capacity for self-regulation. The bilateral stimulation in EMDR helps your brain's natural processing system complete what it couldn't during the original trauma — transforming stuck, intrusive memories into integrated past experiences that no longer hijack your present.
practices: Look for an EMDR-trained therapist (EMDRIA certification is the gold standard)
Ask about their approach to preparation and resourcing before processing
Expect 8-12+ sessions for a single-incident trauma; more for complex trauma

preparation.label: Preparation Phase First
preparation.headline: You may benefit from more preparation before EMDR processing.
preparation.body: Your responses suggest you have some of the foundations for EMDR but may benefit from more preparation work first. This is not a barrier — it's an important part of the EMDR protocol itself. EMDR has a preparation phase specifically designed to build the resources and stability needed for trauma processing. A good EMDR therapist will spend significant time here before moving to processing. The preparation phase is not wasted time — it's the foundation that makes processing safe and effective.
practices: Look for an EMDR therapist who emphasizes the preparation phase
Focus on building regulation resources and a 'safe place' before processing
Be honest with your therapist about your current stability and dissociation levels

complex.label: Complex Trauma — Modified Approach
complex.headline: Your trauma history may require a modified EMDR approach.
complex.body: If you have complex trauma or significant dissociation, standard EMDR protocols may need to be modified. This doesn't mean EMDR can't help — it means you need a therapist with specific complex trauma and dissociation training. Complex trauma EMDR requires more preparation, more titration, and often integration with other approaches like parts work (IFS) or somatic therapy. The right therapist will know how to adapt the protocol to your specific needs.
practices: Look for an EMDR therapist with specific complex trauma and dissociation training
Ask about their experience with C-PTSD
Consider EMDR as part of a broader treatment approach

title: Are Your Physical Symptoms Trauma-Related?
description: Chronic pain, fatigue, digestive issues, and other physical symptoms are often the body's way of expressing unresolved trauma. Find out if your symptoms might have a somatic root.

questions.text: Do you experience chronic pain that hasn't responded fully to medical treatment?

options.label: No chronic pain
Some pain with a clear medical cause
Chronic pain with no clear medical cause
Pain that seems disproportionate to any physical cause

questions.text: Do you experience chronic fatigue that isn't explained by sleep or medical conditions?

options.label: No — my energy is generally okay
Sometimes — fatigue comes and goes
Yes — I'm exhausted no matter how much I sleep
My fatigue is explained by a medical condition

questions.text: Do you experience digestive issues that seem stress or emotion-related?

options.label: No significant digestive issues
Sometimes — there's a stress-gut connection
Yes — my gut is very reactive to stress and emotions
I have digestive issues but they seem purely physical

questions.text: Do your physical symptoms worsen when you're emotionally stressed?

options.label: No clear connection
Sometimes — the connection is there but not always obvious
Yes — there's a very clear mind-body connection in my symptoms
I haven't paid attention to this connection

questions.text: Have you had extensive medical testing that came back normal despite significant symptoms?

options.label: No — my symptoms have clear medical explanations
Some testing has been normal but not all
Yes — doctors can't find a cause for my symptoms
I haven't had extensive testing

questions.text: Do you experience autoimmune conditions or frequent illness?

options.label: No — my immune system seems normal
I get sick more than average
Yes — I have autoimmune issues or chronic illness
I have autoimmune issues but they're well-managed medically

questions.text: Do you experience headaches or migraines that seem connected to stress or emotions?

options.label: No significant headaches
Sometimes — there's a connection
Yes — my headaches are clearly stress or emotion-triggered
I have headaches but they seem purely physical

questions.text: Do you have a history of trauma, adverse childhood experiences, or chronic stress?

options.label: Relatively stable childhood and life
Some difficult experiences but I'm not sure they were traumatic
Yes — significant trauma history
I'd rather not say

questions.text: Have any of your physical symptoms improved during periods of lower stress or emotional healing?

options.label: No — my symptoms don't seem to respond to emotional state
Sometimes — there seems to be a connection
Yes — clear improvement when I'm less stressed or doing healing work
I haven't paid attention to this

questions.text: Do you feel that your physical symptoms are connected to your emotional or psychological history?

options.label: No — they feel purely physical
Maybe — I've wondered about this
Yes — I believe there's a significant connection
I've never thought about this before

low.label: Minimal Somatic Connection
low.headline: Your physical symptoms don't appear to have a strong somatic/trauma root.
low.body: Your responses suggest your physical symptoms are likely primarily medical in nature and don't appear to be significantly driven by unresolved trauma or nervous system dysregulation. Continue working with your healthcare providers. Even primarily medical conditions can be influenced by stress and nervous system state, so maintaining good regulation supports overall health.
practices: Continue working with your healthcare team
Consider stress management practices as supportive care
Stay curious about the mind-body connection

moderate.label: Possible Somatic Component
moderate.headline: Your symptoms may have a somatic component worth exploring.
moderate.body: Your responses suggest there may be a somatic or stress-related component to some of your physical symptoms. Many physical conditions are influenced by stress and nervous system dysregulation, even when they also have clear medical causes. Addressing the somatic component — through nervous system regulation, trauma-informed care, and body-based practices — can improve outcomes even for conditions with known medical causes. This is not about dismissing your physical symptoms; it's about treating the whole person.
practices: Discuss the mind-body connection with your healthcare provider
Consider adding somatic practices to your treatment
Explore whether stress reduction and emotional healing improve your symptoms

high.label: Significant Somatic/Trauma Root
high.headline: Your physical symptoms may have significant somatic and trauma roots.
high.body: Your responses strongly suggest your physical symptoms may be significantly influenced by unresolved trauma, chronic stress, or nervous system dysregulation. This is not 'all in your head' — these are real physical symptoms with real physiological mechanisms. The body keeps the score, literally. Unresolved trauma dysregulates the nervous system, the immune system, the endocrine system, and the gut-brain axis. The symptoms you're experiencing are your body's way of expressing what hasn't been processed. Trauma-informed somatic care can make a real difference.
practices: Seek a trauma-informed healthcare provider who understands the mind-body connection
Consider somatic therapy as part of your treatment approach
Explore resources on trauma and the body
